title: Troubleshooting marbling problems
subtitle: Diagnosing and correcting the most common marbling failures
excerpt: Most marbling problems trace back to one of four variables: size consistency, paint preparation, paper mordanting, or temperature. This reading covers the common failure modes, paint sinking, patterns blurring, pigment not transferring, and colour bleeding, and how to correct each one without starting over.
sourceNotes: Marbling troubleshooting guidance synthesised from established marbling teaching and practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: When a dropped pigment falls through the surface of the marbling size rather than floating. Caused by insufficient ox gall, size that is too thick, or a pigment that has been over-diluted.

Oil or soap residue on the tray or tools that breaks the surface tension of the marbling size and causes the paint to spread out of control or not spread at all. Start with a clean tray every session.

content.text: Marbling is a system of balanced variables. When one variable shifts, it usually affects one specific failure mode. The troubleshooting approach is to change one variable at a time, test on a scrap, and assess before changing another.
Paint sinks to the bottom of the tray

is the most common beginner failure. Causes: (1) Not enough ox gall. Add one more drop per colour and test. (2) The size is too thick. Thin with water; a very thick size holds paint by friction, not flotation. (3) The pigment has been diluted too much and is lighter than the size. Use less water in the pigment.
Paint spreads too far and thins out

Too much ox gall, or a size that is too thin. Reduce the ox gall to one drop per colour. If the problem persists, let the size rest longer (carrageenan size continues to thicken for up to 24 hours after mixing). A warmer room also thins the size.
Pattern blurs when the paper is lifted

Either the paper was not mordanted with alum (the pattern washes off rather than bonding), or the mordanted paper was not fully dry before use. Alum mordanting is not optional: it is the mechanism by which the pigment bonds to the paper. Allow at least 2 hours drying time after mordanting, or overnight.

The tray temperature is too high (above 22°C, the size becomes less viscous and patterns blur), or the colours have too much ox gall relative to the size viscosity. Work at room temperature (18, 22°C). Alternatively, the combed pattern was held too long before laying the paper, the colours begin to merge after 2, 3 minutes at the surface.
Lay a strip of newsprint across the tray surface and lift it away to remove spent paint between each pull. If
from oil on hands or tools has occurred, the surface will show a broken, cratered pattern even before any paint is added. Drain the tray and start with fresh size.
